one_line_takeaway: A practitioner-facing SE4AI experience report cataloguing seven ways RAG pipelines fail in production and arguing that RAG robustness must be earned through runtime calibration rather than designed in up front - useful engineering guidance, not a security study.
key_finding: RAG failures are diverse and mostly cannot be designed out up front: the two headline takeaways are that (1) validation of a RAG system is only feasible during operation (runtime), and (2) robustness evolves through calibration rather than being designed in at the start. Concretely, the case studies surfaced that a larger context window gave more accurate responses (8K vs 4K on the AI Tutor), explicitly noted as contrary to prior GPT-3.5 findings ('Lost in the Middle'); that adding metadata (file name + chunk number) into the retrieved context improved the reader's extraction; that open-source sentence-embedding models performed as well as closed-source ones on small text; and that on the biomedical corpus the automated OpenEvals scorer was more pessimistic than a human rater (with the caveat that the human reviewers were non-experts, so the LLM may know more than they do).
core_contribution: An experience report (SE4AI / software-engineering perspective) that catalogues seven recurring failure points (FP1-FP7) encountered when engineering Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, drawn from three real case studies. The paper's own contributions, as stated, are: (1) a catalogue of RAG failure points; (2) an experience report from three case-study implementations (two running in production at Deakin University); and (3) a research roadmap for RAG in the SE community. The seven failure points are: FP1 Missing Content (question unanswerable from the corpus, system may be fooled into answering anyway), FP2 Missed Top-Ranked Documents (answer exists but not in the top-K returned), FP3 Not in Context / Consolidation-strategy Limitations (retrieved but dropped during consolidation), FP4 Not Extracted (answer in context but LLM fails to extract it, typically due to noise/contradiction), FP5 Wrong Format (LLM ignores format instructions like table/list), FP6 Incorrect Specificity (answer too general or too specific for the user's need), and FP7 Incomplete (answer omits information that was available in context). The authors claim this is the first empirical/experience insight into building robust RAG systems from an SE viewpoint.
threat_model: None. This is not a security paper and defines no adversary. It is an engineering experience report about reliability/quality failure modes of RAG pipelines under normal (non-adversarial) operation. The only security-adjacent observation is a single lesson in Table 2 (tied to FP5-7, AI Tutor case study) noting that 'jailbreaks bypass the RAG system and hit the safety training' and that prior work shows fine-tuning can reverse safety training (citing Lermen et al. 2023, LoRA undoing Llama-2 safety), with the practical recommendation to test all fine-tuned LLMs used in a RAG system. This is a passing operational caveat, not a threat model, attack, or defense evaluated by the paper. No threat model is invented here.
limitations: Self-reported: only one of three case studies (BioASQ) is reproducible/released; the other two are confidential. The AI Tutor pilot findings were preliminary (pilot only commenced late Oct 2023). For BioASQ the human raters were non-domain-experts, a stated threat to validity for the 'automated eval is more pessimistic' finding. Beyond the authors' own caveats: the study is qualitative with no controlled experiments, no ablations, and no baselines - failure points are catalogued, not quantified (no per-FP frequency counts are reported). Generalisability rests on three systems from one institution. NOTE: the paper is internally inconsistent about the BioASQ corpus size - the abstract/intro/conclusion repeatedly cite 'an empirical investigation involving 15,000 documents and 1000 questions,' while Section 4.3 and Table 1 state 4017 downloaded open-access documents (and 1000 questions). This discrepancy is unresolved in the text.

datasets_used: BioASQ (biomedical QA; Section 4.3/Table 1 report 4017 downloaded open-access documents and 1000 question-answer pairs; answer types yes/no, summarisation, factoid, or list; abstract/intro/conclusion instead cite 15,000 documents and 1000 questions - internal discrepancy)
AI Tutor internal corpus (Deakin LMS content; 38 documents; videos, HTML, PDF)
Cognitive Reviewer internal corpus (research PDFs of any size; used by Deakin PhD students)
quantitative_results: BioASQ: 1000 questions run through the pipeline; answers generated with GPT-4 and scored with OpenAI OpenEvals
40 flagged issues manually inspected (plus all OpenEvals-flagged-inaccurate cases)
Automated OpenEvals scoring found more pessimistic than a (non-expert) human rater on the biomedical domain
AI Tutor: larger context improved accuracy, 8K vs 4K, explicitly contrary to prior GPT-3.5 work
AI Tutor pilot: 200-student unit, commenced 30 Oct 2023; system developed Aug-Nov 2023
9 lessons distilled (Table 2), mapped to failure points and case studies
baselines_compared: No quantitative benchmark baselines - this is a qualitative experience report, not a comparative evaluation. Informal comparisons only: open-source vs closed-source sentence-embedding models (reported comparable on small text); 8K vs 4K context window (larger better); RAG vs fine-tuning discussed as design alternatives but not empirically compared; OpenEvals automated scoring vs human rater judgement.
